nativeRange.habitat: Still and slow-moving waters: ponds, lakes, streams, ditches, marshes, and backwaters; highly tolerant of low oxygen, turbidity, and a wide temperature range

decorPreferences: flat stones or PVC tubes as spawning sites for males
live or artificial plants for cover

diet.notes: Highly unselective feeder. Accepts high-quality flake, micro-pellets, spirulina-based foods, algae wafers, frozen or live brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Will graze on algae and biofilm in the tank. A varied diet that includes vegetable matter supports best coloration and health in the rosy-red color morph.

goodWith: other cool-water minnows (e.g., white cloud mountain minnow)
smaller goldfish (fancy goldfish with similar temperature needs)
avoidWith: large predatory fish that can swallow them whole

imageCandidates.notes: Wild-type fathead minnow (Pimephales promelas); shows species body shape clearly. Rosy-red color morph images also available on Wikimedia.
FishBase photo gallery for Pimephales promelas; includes multiple wild specimens.

summary: The Rosy Red Minnow is a captive-bred orange-pink color morph of the North American Fathead Minnow (Pimephales promelas). Extremely cold-tolerant (10–26°C), easy to breed, and undemanding, it suits unheated tanks and outdoor ponds. Widely sold as feeder fish but deserves recognition as an attractive, hardy aquarium species.
careNotes:
The Rosy Red Minnow is simply a selectively-bred color morph of the native North American Fathead Minnow (Pimephales promelas); the two are identical in care. The species is one of the most cold-tolerant aquarium fish available, thriving in unheated tanks at room temperature (around 18–22°C) and tolerating temperatures as low as 10°C, making it well suited to cool-water setups alongside white clouds, goldfish, or hillstream loaches. It also tolerates warmer conditions up to about 26°C for short periods, giving it significant flexibility.

Fathead Minnows are remarkably hardy and forgiving of water quality lapses, including brief low-oxygen events — a trait that makes them popular in scientific toxicology assays where reproducible responses to stressors are required. Despite this toughness, stable water conditions and routine maintenance promote best coloration and longevity.

The species belongs to family Leuciscidae, elevated from Cyprinidae in 2018 (Tan & Armbruster). A school of at least 6 individuals is recommended; males can become mildly territorial around spawning sites but are not a threat to other species. Males are noticeably larger than females, develop tubercles on the snout during breeding, and produce a mucus secretion (pad) on the head used to tend eggs on the underside of flat surfaces. Provide flat rocks, broad leaves, or short lengths of PVC pipe as spawning caves.

Rosy Red color forms may be slightly less robust than wild-type Fathead Minnows due to selective breeding. Avoid keeping with large predators or purely tropical fish requiring temperatures above 28°C consistently.
breedingNotes: One of the easiest freshwater fish to breed. Males establish territories around a flat surface (underside of a stone, piece of slate, or PVC tube) and entice females to deposit adhesive eggs there. The male guards and fans the clutch until hatching (4–7 days depending on temperature). Fry are free-swimming within a day and accept finely crushed flake and infusoria. Multiple clutches can be produced from a single group in one season. Breeding is triggered by rising temperatures in spring and can be induced in aquaria by gradually increasing temperature from ~15°C to ~22°C.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rosy Red Minnow / Fathead Minnow
primary.notes: Primary hobbyist-trade source. Covers care, temperature range (50–75°F / 10–24°C), tank size, diet, compatibility, and notes on the rosy-red as a color morph of P. promelas. Also discusses feeder-fish origin and suitability for unheated tanks.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Pimephales promelas
additional.notes: Detailed species account. Confirms adult size (up to 9 cm TL for males), temperature (10–25°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness, native range across most of North America, breeding behaviour (egg-scatter on underside of substrate with male guarding), and taxonomy (Leuciscidae, Cypriniformes). Notes widespread use in ecotoxicology as a standard test organism.

additional.name: FishBase — Pimephales promelas
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 9.9 cm TL, commonly 6–8 cm), temperature range (4–25°C wild), native range (central North America from Canada to northern Mexico), diet (omnivore: algae, detritus, small invertebrates), and family placement (Leuciscidae). Also confirms status as a principal EPA ecotoxicology test species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Cool-water fish for unheated aquariums
additional.notes: Discusses Fathead / Rosy Red Minnow as an underrated unheated-tank option; confirms peaceful temperament, schooling behavior, ease of breeding, and broad temperature tolerance (similar to white cloud minnows). Recommends groups of 6+.

additional.name: Tan M. & Armbruster J.W. (2018) — Revised nomenclature for North American leuciscids (Cypriniformes: Cyprinidae: Leuciscidae), Zootaxa 4495(1):139–147
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed paper establishing Leuciscidae as a full family elevated from Cyprinidae, directly affecting the family placement of Pimephales promelas. Cited to document the 2018 reclassification referenced in the species brief.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard eggs on underside of flat surface; no special equipment needed. Fry accept micro-crushed flake from free-swimming stage. Multiple clutches per season typical.
